paper_title: Rethinking RAG in Long Videos: What to Retrieve and How to Use It?

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Retrieval-augmented generation is moving beyond text into long, egocentric video, where systems must select query-relevant chunks across multiple modalities and temporal granularities. Yet progress in VideoRAG is limited by two gaps: existing benchmarks allow queries to be answered without the video, obscuring retrieval errors, and prior methods apply a single modality-granularity configuration per query, ignoring chunk-level variability. We address both by introducing V-RAGBench, a benchmark of $\langle$query, evidence chunk, answer$\rangle$ triplets that enables faithful, decoupled evaluation of retrieval and generation, and CARVE, a simple method that runs parallel retrievers across configurations and employs chunk-adaptive reranking to identify the winning configuration for each chunk. Each chunk then enters the generator under its winning configuration selected during retrieval, yielding an interleaved evidence form where the chunk-level decision propagates across both stages. CARVE outperforms eight recent VideoRAG baselines, with the chunks supplied to the generator interleaving multiple configurations rather than sharing a single one, a behavior unattainable by query-level methods.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) grounds large language models (LLMs) in external knowledge, improving factual reliability [13, 22, 56, 86]. Its frontier is now moving beyond static text corpora toward video-centric settings [28, 77, 84, 71], where the primary source of knowledge is no longer curated text but long-form video in the egocentric setting, as wearable devices turn daily life into long first-person videos [6, 73] and agentic systems operate directly on accumulated personal video data [8, 51]. We refer to this setting, RAG over long videos, as VideoRAG, where the system needs to retrieve query-relevant evidence from long video. This shift does not merely extend retrieval but fundamentally increases its retrieval and generation complexity. Given target query, the system must retrieve query-relevant video chunks by matching across multimodal representations [30, 45, 81], such as visual features and higher-level textual abstractions, as well as across multiple temporal granularities [23, 40], from frame-level details to clip-level segments. The problem thus moves beyond what to retrieve to deciding "which modality and which granularity to retrieve from?" and "how to exploit them jointly across retrieval and generation?" Despite this growing interest [39, 44, 58], current benchmark datasets have not kept pace. While hour-scale source videos are available through Ego4D [19] and EgoLife [73], the queries paired with them are scarce and were not designed for VideoRAG. In particular, recent work [37] shows that over half of widely used video QA samples can be answered without the video at all, relying on linguistic priors, world knowledge, or static cues. Such queries are fatal for VideoRAG, since system can reach correct answers even when retrieval surfaces irrelevant chunks, so high generation accuracy does not imply successful retrieval, and final accuracy fails as proxy for the retrieval stage. Under such evaluation, the right way to use modality and temporal granularity across retrieval and generation cannot be identified, as each design choice is invisible behind the final accuracy. The same gap appears on the methodological side. Recent work proposes techniques for VideoRAG, such as guided query refinement [66, 71], modality fusion [42, 74], and query decomposition [70], while others delegate retrieval to iterative agentic loops [44, 77] that act more as test-time scaling Figure 1: The overview of CARVE: Stage 1 builds candidate pool via chunk-wise parallel retrieval, while Stage 2 performs chunk-adaptive reranking. The final top-k evidence with their winning configuration is passed to the generator in modality-interleaved form. than as principled retrieval. Although retrieval is the core component on which all these frameworks ultimately rest, its validation is left at shallow level, reduced to downstream QA accuracy on the full pipeline. This is in sharp contrast to text-centric RAG, where retrieval is treated as first-class object of study and audited through stage-wise metrics on query-evidence pairs [13, 17, 34]. We address this gap by introducing V-RAGBench, dedicated benchmark designed to make retrieval and generation in VideoRAG faithfully measurable both in isolation and in their interaction. V-RAGBench consists of query, evidence chunk, answer triplets drawn from long egocentric videos in Ego4D [19] and EgoLife [73], totaling 2,100 high-quality triplets across 216 videos spanning 19 hours. Targeting the VideoRAG setting, V-RAGBench jointly enforces three properties that no existing dataset satisfies together: each query is (i) tied to non-recurring evidence, so that the underlying event does not repeat elsewhere in the video as near-duplicate; (ii) visually-grounded, so that the answer is not implied by the query itself or recoverable from parametric knowledge alone; and (iii) evidence-localized, so that the answer cannot be reconstructed from generic visual cues that incidentally recur across non-evidence chunks. Property (i) is enforced via temporal segmentation and clustering, while (ii) and (iii) are enforced by dedicated post-hoc filters. Together, these properties make generation causally dependent on retrieval, enabling stage-wise view of VideoRAG. With V-RAGBench in hand, we shift the main question of VideoRAG from improving QA accuracy to which representation of video chunk best supports each stage of the pipeline, namely retrieval and generation. Each chunk admits four candidate configurations1, formed by crossing two axes: modality (visual embeddings vs. embeddings of textual summaries) and temporal granularity (frame-level vs. clip-level). Existing retrieval strategies for VideoRAG [21, 22, 28, 53] apply single configuration, or fixed fusion of the four, uniformly to every candidate chunk given querya query-level decision. However, this is suboptimal because the best configuration is property of the chunks content, not of the query; visually salient moment is most discriminable through visual embeddings, while semantically rich moment is better surfaced through its textual summary, and granularity follows the same content dependence. We therefore propose CARVE (Chunk-Aware Reranking for Video Evidence) in Figure 1, novel framework that exploits multi-modal cross-encoder signals to assign each candidate chunk its winning configuration based on its query relevance. The key idea is to re-purpose reranking, which is tool widely used in text-RAG [1, 36, 64] but largely unexplored in VideoRAG, as mechanism for chunk-level configuration decision. Concretely, CARVE operates in two stages. First, parallel retrieval runs four parallel retrievers, one per configuration, each contributing its top-k chunks to shared candidate pool. Second, multimodal cross-encoder re-scores each candidate under its retrieving configuration (e.g., by querytext relevance for textual retrievals and by query-visual relevance for visual retrievals). Sorting these configuration-specific scores yields the final ranking, in which chunks from different configurations are interleaved and each chunk carries its retrieving configuration onward. The final top-k chunks then serve both stages of the VideoRAG pipeline. For retrieval, they are the output of CARVE, where the chunk-level decision lies in which configuration scored each chunk highest. For generation, the same chunks are augmented to the query, but each is supplied to the generator under its retrieving configuration, so the representation fed to the generator varies per chunk. Importantly, our experiments reveal that this chunk-level selection is not only beneficial at the retrieval stage, but becomes even more impactful when carried into generation, leading to further gains in answer accuracy. 1Other retrieval signals, such as video knowledge graphs [16, 26, 55] or metadata (e.g., explicit temporal clue) [20, 52], can aid retrieval but are not consistently available. We focus on content-based representations, which are universally accessible. 2 Our experiments on V-RAGBench reveal four key findings: (1) CARVE significantly outperforms eight baseline methods on both retrieval and generation, comparison enabled by V-RAGBenchs stage-wise measurability; (2) modalitygranularity ablation reveals large performance gaps across configurations at both stages, with no single configuration uniformly optimal, motivating chunk-level rather than query-level decisions; (3) CARVEs chunk-level decisions distribute fairly evenly across the four configurations, showing that per-chunk diversity is genuine rather than collapsing onto single dominant choice; and (4) at generation, CARVE even surpasses trained query-level router that learns to pick one configuration per query, without any additional training.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Retrieval-Augmented Generation in Text. RAG improves generation by retrieving query-relevant evidence from an external text corpus and conditioning the generator on it [2, 13, 29, 34]. Progress has centered on retrieval, since retrieval quality largely dictates final-answer quality. One axis advances the retriever, from sparse and dense retrievers [17, 31, 72] to hybrid sparsedense pipelines that combine complementary signals [22, 46, 54]. second reformulates the query through rewriting [43, 63, 87] or expansion [4, 13, 83] to make retrieval intent explicit. third addresses the limited precision of first-stage retrievers through reranking, where cross-encoder re-scores candidates [1, 36, 64, 78]. Together, these advances substantially improve retrieval quality, with gains that largely translate into downstream generation accuracy. They are, however, developed for text and do not readily generalize to retrieving evidence from video. Retrieval-Augmented Generation in Long Video. Long-form video understanding has traditionally relied on universal video representations such as sparse frame sampling [7, 38, 75], visual-token compression [12, 24, 60, 82], or full-video textualization [32, 35, 41, 81]. Yet, these encode the entire video into query-independent view, diluting query-relevant evidence as length grows. This motivates VideoRAG, which mirrors text RAG by retrieving query-relevant evidence before generation. The main challenge in VideoRAG is that retrieval and generation become substantially more complex than in text. video chunk is continuous in time and inherently multimodal [30, 45, 81], expressible through visual frames or derived textual abstractions, and at temporal granularities from individual frames to segments [23, 40]. Existing work explores these axes from complementary angles. On the modality side, Jeong et al. [28] retrieves chunks using multimodal embeddings, while Luo et al. [42] augments retrieval with visually-aligned auxiliary text such as OCR and ASR. On the granularity side, hierarchical and tree-structured representations index the video at multiple temporal scales [53, 65]. separate line replaces one-shot retrieval with iterative, agentic probing, where an LLM repeatedly fetches frames or segments to refine its evidence [8, 62, 71, 77]. However, these methods commit to single modalitygranularity choice (or fixed fusion of all of them) per query, and analysis is weighted toward downstream QA accuracy, leaving retrieval quality itself less directly examined. As we discuss next, this stems from the benchmark side, where existing datasets are not designed to evaluate retrieval separately from generation. Our work addresses both gaps by treating modality and granularity as explicit design axes selected at the chunk-level, and by introducing benchmark that makes retrieval and generation independently measurable. Video Benchmark Datasets for QA. Video QA benchmarks have progressed from short third-person clips [27, 33, 69, 79] toward longer videos and, increasingly, egocentric settings [15, 19, 47, 48, 73]. This shift mirrors the natural target of VideoRAG: personal video has emerged as primary retrieval domain, as wearable devices accumulate hour-scale first-person video and personal agents increasingly operate over it [51, 88]. However, existing benchmarks remain limited in this regime. For example, EgoSchema (3-minute clips) [47] QaEgo4D (short Ego4D intervals) [15], Video-MME (60 min) [18], and LongVideoBench (1 hour) [67]. While Ego4D [19] and EgoLife [73] provide hour-scale source video, their accompanying queries fall short on two counts. First, public queries are scarce and serve different evaluation goals. Ego4Ds queries are scarce and belong to the Episodic Memory benchmark, which evaluates retrieval alone, while EgoLifeQA targets long-term memory aggregation across hours and days. Second, neither targets VideoRAG evaluation, and thus lacks the three properties it requires: (i) non-recurring evidence, (ii) visual grounding, and (iii) evidence localization. These ensure that retrieval and generation can be assessed independently and jointly, with correct answers tied to the labeled evidence rather than to spurious cues. CG-Bench [9] and MM-Lifelong [10] make partial progress by checking shortcut 3 answerability and clue grounding, addressing (ii) but leaving (i) and (iii) untouched. Our benchmark closes this gap by jointly enforcing all three properties on hour-scale egocentric video.

sections.title: 3 Formulating VideoRAG along Two Design Axes
sections.content: We formalize VideoRAG as retrieve-then-generate process over long videos. While prior studies [28, 53, 77] establish this pipeline, two design choices remain underspecified. First, the same chunk admits multiple representations across modality (visual vs. textual) and granularity (frame vs. clip), but these are typically combined at fixed scales rather than treated as decision variables. Second, the relationship between the representation used for retrieval and the one used for generation has received little attention, with the two typically tied to single choice by default. Following standard text RAG practice [2, 56], VideoRAG system partitions long video into nonoverlapping, fixed-interval temporal chunks that serve as retrieval units, yielding = {v1, . . . , vV }. We default to 2-minute chunks, matching the empirical 23 minute span of bounded semantic content in long-form video [25]. Each such chunk, however, is fundamentally richer than text passage: admits multiple representations ϕm,g(v) along two complementary axes: modality {vis, text} (visual features vs. textual abstractions) and temporal granularity {frame, clip} (framelevel details vs. clip-level summaries).2 Given query q, retriever matches against chunk representations to select candidate chunks Vq , and an MLLM-based generator produces an answer from those chunks under chosen representation: (cid:98)a = G(cid:0)q, {ϕmg,gg (v)}vVq (cid:1) where Vq = R(q, {ϕmr,gr (v)}vV ) , (1) (mr, gr) and (mg, gg) are the configurations chosen for retrieval and generation, respectively; and ϕm,g(v) is the representation of chunk under modality and granularity g. The goal is twofold: Vq should contain the chunk that supports the answer, and (cid:98)a should match the reference answer a. Notably, whereas text chunk has single canonical form, video chunk forces VideoRAG to decide which (m, g) to use for retrieval and which for generation, and these decisions need not coincide. We now describe how the two design axes are instantiated in this work. Full implementation details (e.g., decoding hyperparameters, temperature) are deferred to Appendix D, and the prompt templates used are provided in Appendix G. Chunk Modality. The modality axis {vis, text} exposes chunk through two complementary channels. The visual modality encodes as raw visual features extracted by an MLLM-based multimodal encoder (Qwen3-VL-Embedding-2B [5] by default), preserving fine-grained appearance and motion cues that resist verbalization. In contrast, the textual modality describes in natural language by prompting an MLLM (Qwen3-VL-8B) to summarize the chunk v, and embeds the resulting text with the same encoder, surfacing semantic structure (e.g., what is happening, where, and with whom) at the cost of compressing pixel-level detail. Neither modality is universally preferable; the more useful one depends on the query and chunk content, as textual abstractions surface semantics invisible to raw pixels, while visual features capture appearance and motion. Chunk Granularity. Even within fixed modality, the temporal scope at which chunk is read can vary. The granularity axis {frame, clip} exposes chunk at two contrasting scales. The frame-level retains fine-grained, per-frame information through small set of keyframes. Specifically, we cluster all frame embeddings in the chunk with k-means++ [3] (n = 5 by default) and take the cluster centroids as keyframes, covering distinct visual states rather than uniform time samples. On the other hand, the clip-level instead treats as single unit. They are then realized in modality-specific way. Under the visual modality, the frame-level meanpools the keyframe embeddings while the clip-level encodes the entire chunk into one embedding. Under the textual modality, the same MLLM produces summary capped at 512 tokens, applied per keyframe (concatenated before embedding) for the frame-level and over the entire chunk for the clip-level. This cap acts as an upper bound that accommodates detailed clip descriptions used in prior multimodal summarization work [11, 25] while preventing verbose outputs from dominating retrieval and generation context. Neither granularity is universally preferable; frame-level forms capture transient details but lose the surrounding flow, while clip-level forms retain temporal context but blur transient details, so the more useful scale depends on the query and the chunk content. 2We instantiate and with two values each, giving four (m, g) configurations for ease of exposition. Our formulation readily accommodates additional modalities and granularities. 4 Scope and Objective. With (m, g) now made explicit, two questions follow: how it should be decided and at what level. In other words, for each chunk v, which representation ϕm,g(v) should be passed to the retriever and the generator? The simplest choice is to fix one (m, g) per query and apply it uniformly across chunks [28, 53]; agentic variants [77] issue multiple retrievals per query, but each call still commits to single (m, g). This leaves room for finer-grained control, since the most useful (m, g) may differ across chunks even within single query. For instance, identifying specific object visible in only one keyframe is best handled by frame-level visual embedding, while describing how an activity unfolds over the chunk is better expressed by clip-level textual summary. We therefore explore deciding (m, g) per chunk, carrying the choice consistently into both retrieval and generation while keeping the standard retrieve-then-generate pipeline intact.

sections.title: 4 Construction of V-RAGBench
sections.content: Diagnosing how the two design axes interact at retrieval and generation requires faithful benchmark in which the two stages are measurable both in isolation and in their interaction, which in turn requires every query to be tied to uniquely sufficient evidence chunk. We therefore construct V-RAGBENCH, benchmark of query, evidence chunk, answer triplets built from hour-scale egocentric videos through the four stages (see Figure 2)namely source selection, event extraction and deduplication, query generation, and filteringdescribed below. Our construction process is further verified by human study (see Section C.5): on 577 sampled QA pairs, annotators show substantial inter-annotator agreement (Cohens κ = 0.63), jointly flagging only 3.1% as unanswerable. Source Video Selection. We focus on egocentric video as the source domain for VideoRAG. Unlike third-person long videos (e.g., movies, lectures) organized around single narrative arc, first-person streams capture continuous, weakly structured daily activity in which the same actor revisits similar scenes and routines, making retrieval over personal memory both more realistic and substantially harder. Thus, we draw source videos from two complementary egocentric corpora, Ego4D [19] and EgoLife [73], retaining only videos longer than one hour and applying stratified sampling over length bins so that the corpus spans wide range of durations. This yields search corpus of 216 personal videos in total, comprising 42 EgoLife recordings (avg. 379.1 min., 177.8552.7 min.) and 174 Ego4D videos (avg. 86.0 min., 60.0423.6 min.); see Table 6. Reflecting the personal nature of VideoRAG, retrieval is performed within each wearers own corpus, so every query is grounded in their video history. Per-domain and per-length statistics are in Appendix C.1. Event Extraction and Deduplication. This stage converts each long video into small set of distinct event chunks that form non-redundant evidence pool for QA generation. For each video, we encode every sampled frame with EVA02-E-14-plus [57] (a CLIP-based vision encoder) and apply kernel temporal segmentation [49] on the resulting frame embeddings to cut the video into semantically coherent, time-contiguous segments. The number of segments is capped at 150 for the shorter Ego4D videos and 900 for the day-long EgoLife recordings, with each segment constrained to 5300s. We then re-embed each segment as whole with Qwen3-VL-Embedding-8B [5] in video mode and cluster the embeddings with k-Means++ (k = 50 for Ego4D, = 300 for EgoLife), retaining only the segment closest to each centroid as its representative. This suppresses recurring routines (e.g., repeated meal preparation), leaving chunks that correspond to distinct eventsa prerequisite for QA generation, since queries grounded in recurring events would admit multiple sufficient evidence chunks; while natural in the real world, such queries are excluded here for rigorous VideoRAG evaluation. Details of deduplication and statistics of this stage are in appendix C.4. Query Generation. For each event chunk, we prompt Gemini-3-flash-preview to generate up to three candidate queries spanning three predefined categories (See Appendix C.3), with the model itself deciding which are applicable rather than forcing all three. The prompt further enforces contextual localization, requiring every query to embed anchor information from its source event so that the query alone describes the situation enough to be matched against the right chunk; this lets V-RAGBench dispense with direct temporal cues such as timestamps or when phrases, forcing retrieval to rely purely on content (full prompt and examples in Appendix G). Each generation produces triple q, [ts, te], of query, evidence time frames, and open-ended form rather than multiple-choice option, ensuring that downstream evaluation reflects the generators true ability rather than being inflated by random guessing over fixed choice set. This yields 67,370 candidate query, evidence chunk, answer triples for the filtering pipeline. 5 Post-hoc Filtering. While event deduplication enforces content uniqueness at the event level, the generated queries are natural-language artifacts whose grounding and uniqueness must be verified at the query level. Specifically, the two properties visual groundingthe answer is not implied by the query or recoverable from its priorsand evidence localizationthe answer is not recoverable from non-evidence chunksare not yet guaranteed and must be enforced post-hoc. We thus apply five filters targeting these properties along with query-level redundancy. (1) Semantic Similarity: queries too close in Qwen3-VL-8B embedding space are removed, with per-video caps of 50 (Ego4D) and 300 (EgoLife) to prevent any single video from dominating; (2) Answerability: GPT-5.2-chat is asked whether each query is answerable from its source clip, and unanswerable ones are removed; (3) Shortcut Bias: queries GPT-5.2-chat answers correctly without visual context are discarded; (4) Empirical Answerability: Gemini-2.5-flash must produce correct answer from the source clip, judged by an LLM-as-a-judge; and (5) Evidence Uniqueness: for each query, we retrieve the top-10 chunks while excluding the true evidence, and discard the query if Gemini-2.5-flash can still answer correctly from this non-evidence set, indicating the answer is recoverable elsewhere. The surviving queries are (1) lexically and semantically distinct, (2, 4) grounded in sufficient evidence, (3) free of parametric and commonsense shortcuts, and (5) not recoverable from any other chunk of the same video. Since the three query categories are unevenly populated after filtering, we randomly subsample each category to match the smallest one (n = 700 per category), yielding balanced dataset of 2,100 queries in total (1,800 train, 300 test).3 We defer per-stage details to Appendix C.5.

sections.title: 5 CARVE: Chunk-Aware Reranking for Video Evidence
sections.content: We now introduce CARVE, novel VideoRAG method that decides the modalitygranularity configuration (m, g) at the chunk-level rather than at the query-level, and propagates that decision consistently from retrieval into generation. Concretely, CARVE operates in two simple stages, namely parallel candidate pooling and chunk-adaptive reranking, as illustrated in Figure 1. Stage 1: Parallel Candidate Pooling. Given query q, this stage builds candidate pool by performing four parallel retrievals, one per configuration (m, g) with {vis, text} and {frame, clip}. The four embeddings ϕm,g(v) of video chunk defined in Eq. (1), one per configuration, are precomputed offline for every chunk in the corpus, so each retrieval at inference time only requires encoding the query into an embedding and performing fast nearest-neighbor lookup against the precomputed chunk embeddings. That is, no additional chunk-encoding cost is incurred in this stage, regardless of how many configurations are queried in parallel. For each configuration (m, g), we compute the dot-product similarity , between the query embedding and each chunk embedding ϕm,g(v), and keep the chunks with the highest scores as the top-k retrieval list C(m,g)(q) for that configuration: C(m,g)(q) = top-k chunks ranked by q, ϕm,g(v). (2) The four lists are then merged into configuration-tagged candidate pool (q), where each retrieved chunk is paired with the configuration (m, g) that retrieved the chunk v: (q) = (cid:91) (cid:110) (cid:0) (m,g) (cid:124)(cid:123)(cid:122)(cid:125) chunk , (m, g) (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) tag (cid:1) : C(m,g)(q) (cid:111) . (3) The same chunk may appear under multiple tags if it was retrieved by more than one configuration. These tags are preserved into Stage 2 rather than collapsed away, since they are precisely the perconfiguration signal that lets the next stage decide winning (m, g) for each chunk. Stage 2: Chunk-Adaptive Reranking. Given the candidate pool P(q) from Stage 1, this stage selects the final top-k chunks, while assigning each surviving chunk single winning configuration (m, g). Since the chunk embeddings in P(q) live in different modalities depending on the tagtextual when = text, visual when = viswe use multi-modal cross-encoder CE(, )4 that jointly attends to textual query and either textual or visual chunk modality. Empirically, this propagation from retrieval to generationnot the retrieval gain aloneis where CARVEs strongest improvements appear.

sections.title: 6 Evaluation on V-RAGBench
sections.content: Our evaluation of CARVE on the test set of V-RAGBench consists of three parts. First, we compare CARVE against recent VideoRAG methods on both retrieval and generation to demonstrate its superiority  (Table 1)  . Second, we study how modality and temporal granularity should be selected under our method, showing that CARVEs chunk-level choices outperform query-level alternatives  (Table 2)  . Third, we further show the efficiency and effectiveness of chunk-level decisions via latency, selection distribution, and comparison against two trained query-level routers (Tables 35). Compared Methods. We compare CARVE against eight RAG methods applicable to VideoRAG, which differ in modality, granularity, and fusion strategy. We group them into two categories. (i) System-level methods apply fixed policy regardless of the query, including VideoRAG-A [28], which linearly interpolates text and visual modality scores; GQR [59], which refines query embeddings at test time via complementary retriever signals; and the multimodal encoders, namely Freeret [89] and GME [85], which retrieve in single embedding space. (ii) Query-level methods adapt the policy per query but apply the same decision to all chunks, including RRF [50], which expands query into variants and fuses retrieval ranks; DAT [22], which dynamically weights dense and sparse retrievers via LLM-assessed reference scores; and VideoRAG-B [42] and UniversalRAG(-LoRA) [76], which respectively decompose or route query across auxiliary databases spanning different modalities and granularities, where (-LoRA) denotes its LoRA-trained variant on the training set of V-RAGBench. For fairness, all baselines share the same chunk embeddings as CARVE and differ only in how representations are selected or combined. See Appendix D.2 for implementation details. Evaluation Metrics. We evaluate retrieval and generation separately. For retrieval, we report two metrics: nDCG@k, which rewards methods that rank the annotated evidence higher; and Recall@k, which measures whether the annotated evidence appears among the top-k retrieved chunks. Since retrieval relies on deterministic embedding similarity, these metrics carry no run-to-run variance. For generation, we report the aggregate pass rate, defined as the fraction of queries whose generated answer is judged correct against the reference by an LLM-as-a-judge (Qwen3.6-35B-A3B), which is necessary since answers are in open-ended form rather than multiple choice (see the prompt in Figure 8). To assess generalization across generators, we evaluate each retrieval method with three backbones: {Qwen3-VL-8B, Qwen3-VL-32B, and Gemma-4-26B}. We fix = 5 throughout experiments, except in Appendix F.4 where we vary to show that CARVE consistently outperforms baselines. Detailed definitions of all metrics are provided in Appendix D.3. 7 Table 1: Retrieval and generation performance on V-RAGBench across system-, query-, and chunklevel methods for VideoRAG. sections.title: 6.1 Main Result: Performance on Retrieval and Generation
sections.content: Table 1 reports retrieval and generation performance on V-RAGBench, comparing CARVE against four system-level and five query-level baselines. On retrieval, CARVE achieves the best Recall@5 (0.603) and nDCG@5 (0.433), exceeding the strongest baseline (VideoRAG-A: 0.510 at Recall@5; GQR: 0.340 at nDCG@5). Notably, this retrieval gain consistently translates into stronger generation across all three generator backbones. With Qwen3-VL-8B, CARVE reaches pass rate of 0.357, outperforming the strongest baseline (VideoRAG-B: 0.315); with Qwen3-VL-32B, CARVE again leads at 0.367 over VideoRAG-A and VideoRAG-B (both at 0.317); and on different model family, Gemma-4-26B, CARVE retains the top score at 0.320, ahead of VideoRAG-A (0.307). These results confirm CARVEs chunk-level decision strengthens both stages: at retrieval, chunk-adaptive reranking surfaces more accurate evidence chunks via each chunks winning configuration; at generation, the same winning configuration propagates as chunk-level interleaved evidence form that is consistently better consumed across generator scales and families. Further analyses are in the appendix: persource breakdown over Ego4D and EgoLife showing the same trend (Appendix F.2), and case study on retrieval-success but generation-failure queries (Appendix F.3). Configuration R@5 nDCG@5 Pass Rate Latency Table 2: Ablation over modalitygranularity configurations in CARVE; performance improves as more configurations are considered. natural question is whether multiple configurations genuinely synergize in CARVE, or whether single dominant one already captures most of the gain. To answer this, we ablate CARVE by varying which modalitygranularity configurations are made available across both parallel retrieval and chunk-adaptive reranking. Table 2 reports the result, where the top block restricts CARVE to single configuration, the middle block expands along one axis while fixing the other, and the bottom row uses all four. In detail, the best single configuration is visual cliplevel at 0.507 Recall@5, the best partial combination is textual frame-and-clip at 0.567, and the full setting reaches 0.603 Recall@5, 0.433 nDCG@5, and 0.357 pass rate. Yet, in some cases, combining configurations is not always beneficial: the visual frame-and-clip combination drops to 0.497 Recall@5, even below visual clip-level alone (0.507). This suggests expanding across modalities is more dependable source of synergy than expanding granularity within single modality. m={text, vis}, g={frame} m={text, vis}, g={clip} m={text}, g={frame, clip} m={vis}, g={frame, clip} m={text}, g={frame} m={text}, g={clip} m={vis}, g={frame} m={vis}, g={clip} m={text, vis}, g={frame, clip} 0.603 0.513 0.543 0.567 0.497 0.430 0.433 0.477 0.507 0.301 0.293 0.336 0.369 0.352 0.398 0.413 0. 0.207 0.157 0.323 0.293 0.303 0.300 0.217 0.327 2.5s 1.5s 5.2s 7.3s 5.0s 5.5s 3.3s 6.1s 0.433 0. 4.6s Importantly, this synergy comes at reasonable latency cost. The full setting runs at 4.6s per query, which is modestly above text-only configurations (1.53.3s) but actually faster than visual-only ones (5.27.3s). This is because each chunk is rendered through its winning configuration, so visual evidence is passed only for chunks that benefit from it, not every retrieved chunk. 6.2 In-depth Analysis of Interleaved Chunk Representations We further analyze how CARVEs interleaved chunk representations work in practice from three angles: the distribution of winning configurations across ranks, the reranking strategy itself, and comparison against trained query-level routers at the generation stage. Table 3: Distribution of winning configuration over chunk-aware reranking. Distribution of Winning Configurations. Table 3 shows the distribution of winning configurations across the top-5 ranked chunks. The distribution does not collapse onto single dominant configuration. At every rank, all four configurations are selected with non-trivial frequency, with {vis, clip} and {text, frame} most often winning, while {text, clip} and {vis, frame} also contribute consistently. This confirms that CARVEs interleaving is genuine. Different chunks are best surfaced through different modalitygranularity configurations, supporting our design choice of treating both axes as chunk-level decisions. {text, frame} {text, clip} {vis, frame} {vis, clip} Configuration Rank 1 Rank 2 Rank 3 Rank 4 Rank 5 0.27 0.13 0.26 0.34 0.27 0.11 0.27 0. 0.25 0.11 0.28 0.36 0.37 0.15 0.24 0.24 0.25 0.13 0.28 0.34 Method Table 4: Reranking configuration choice under CARVE w. Qwen3-VL-2B-Rerank. Reranking Configuration Strategy. We next examine whether CARVEs reranking rule of rescoring each candidate under its retrieving configuration in Eqs. (4)(5) is the right strategy. Table 4 compares against three alternatives: (i) applying single fixed configuration to all candidates regardless of how they were retrieved, (ii) assigning configuration randomly to each candidate (Random), and (iii) feeding all four configurations jointly into the reranker without selection (Concatenation). CARVE substantially outperforms all alternatives. This shows that scoring each chunk under its retrieving configuration tag, rather than under fixed or heuristic alternative, is what drives the gain. In particular, Concatenation fails to match CARVE despite using all four configurations jointly, indicating that the chunk-level alignment between retrieval and reranking matters more than access to more representations. m={text}, g={frame} m={text}, g={clip} m={vis}, g={frame} m={vis}, g={clip} Random Concatenation 0.497 0.490 0.547 0.560 0.343 0.339 0.391 0.396 0.8s 0.5s 4.1s 8.0s CARVE (Ours) 0.312 0.339 0.513 0.513 nDCG@5 3.1s 8.2s Latency 0. 0.433 R@5 3.4s CARVE also achieves favorable accuracylatency trade-off. Reranking visual features is costly due to the larger token count (4.1s and 8.0s for visual-only configurations), but by passing only each chunks winning configuration, CARVE runs at 3.4s while still achieving the best retrieval, substantially faster than visual-only and Concatenation (8.2s). The same trend for retrieval performance and latency holds under different reranker backbone (LamRA-Rank-7B), as seen in Appendix F.5. Method Table 5: Comparison of CARVE against query-level routing in generation. Comparison against Query-Level Routing. We finally examine whether CARVEs chunk-level interleaving of representations fed to the generator is more effective than rendering all retrieved chunks under single, query-level representation. Table 5 compares CARVE with two baselines: (i) single-fixed routing, which always renders chunks under the same configuration regardless of the query, and (ii) trained query-level routers, which select one configuration per query and apply it uniformly to all chunks for generation. The latter includes non-LLM router (an MLP head over frozen BGE-large-en-v1.5 encoder) and an LLM router (Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct fine-tuned with LoRA), both trained on the V-RAGBench training split (see training details in Appendix D.2). CARVE outperforms all baselines despite using no training, while the routers are fine-tuned on the V-RAGBench training split. This shows that no single configuration suits every chunk in query, limitation CARVE sidesteps by varying the representation chunk by chunk through the same reranking step, at competitive generation latency. m={text}, g={frame} m={text}, g={clip} m={vis}, g={frame} m={vis}, g={clip} Non-LLM Router w. sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: In this paper, we address two emerging gaps in VideoRAG. On the benchmark side, we introduce V-RAGBench, collection of query, evidence chunk, answer triplets over hour-scale egocentric videos that jointly enforce non-recurring evidence, visual grounding, and evidence localization for decoupled evaluation of retrieval and generation. On the methodological side, CARVE reframes modality and temporal granularity as chunk-level decision via parallel candidate pooling and chunkadaptive reranking, propagating each chunks winning configuration into generation. On V-RAGBench, CARVE outperforms eight recent baselines for VideoRAG on both stages; and on generation side, it even surpasses the trained query-level routers without additional training, with winning configurations distributing evenly across all configurations rather than collapsing onto dominant one.

sections.title: A Limitations
sections.content: We outline three limitations of this work, which also point to natural directions for future research. Scope of modality and granularity axes. We instantiate the chunk-level configuration space along two axesmodality (visual vs. textual) and granularity (frame vs. clip)yielding four configurations. This covers the most universally available content-based representations and is sufficient to demonstrate that chunk-level decisions outperform query-level ones. By design, CARVE is agnostic to the size of the configuration space: parallel candidate pooling and chunk-adaptive reranking extend naturally to additional modalities and finer granularities without architectural changes, and we leave systematic study of this scaling behavior to future work. One-shot retrieve-then-generate pipeline. We deliberately keep the standard one-shot retrievethen-generate structure intact to isolate the contribution of chunk-level configuration decisions and enable clean comparison against systemand query-level baselines. This means we do not explore iterative or agentic retrieval loops, where evidence could be progressively refined across multiple turns. CARVEs chunk-level winning configurations are natural per-step signal for such loops, and integrating chunk-adaptive reranking into agentic VideoRAG frameworks is promising avenue. Egocentric video as the primary evaluation domain. V-RAGBench is deliberately built on egocentric video from Ego4D [19] and EgoLife [73], since first-person streams are the most realistic and challenging target for VideoRAG. This focused scope is strength for studying the core retrieval problem, but it does not directly evaluate domains such as instructional videos, films, or third-person broadcasts, where narrative structure or production cues may shift the balance among modalitygranularity configurations. Since the three benchmark properties and the CARVE pipeline are domain-agnostic, extending both to other domains is promising direction.

sections.title: B Societal Impact
sections.content: We do not foresee specific societal risks arising directly from the proposed method or benchmark beyond those generally associated with multimodal retrieval and video understanding research. Nevertheless, we acknowledge that egocentric video inherently captures personal, daily-life activities, and any downstream deployment of VideoRAG systems should consider privacy, consent, and responsible use of personal video data. For the human evaluation components described in Appendix C.5 and Appendix D.4, we recruited graduate-student annotators to verify the reliability of our filtering pipeline and the LLM-as-a-judge evaluation. All annotators were compensated above the U.S. federal minimum wage, were informed of the task scope and purpose in advance, and participated voluntarily. No personally identifiable information about the annotators is disclosed in the dataset, paper, or supplementary materials, and their contributions are used solely for the validation analyses reported in this work.

sections.title: C Dataset
sections.content: The overall pipeline of V-RAGBench construction is described in Figure 2. C.1 Dataset statistics V-RAGBench, is benchmark for egocentric video understanding constructed from two complementary sources: long-form daily-life recordings from EgoLife and diverse activity-centric videos from Ego4D. The dataset comprises 2,100 queries grounded in 216 uncut source videos. Detailed dataset statistics, including the distribution of queries across source datasets, categories, domains, and evidence chunk durations, are summarized in Table 6. C.2 Definitions of the source video domains Ego4D videos cover heterogeneous activities, while EgoLife videos are uniformly daily-life. We group Ego4D videos into five high-level domains Domestic, Social, Stationary, Manual, and Outdoor by collapsing the Ego4D metadata categories. For each in-distribution domain we sample 36 training and 6 test videos based on the within-domain length distribution; the Outdoor domain is Figure 2: Overview of the V-RAGBench Construction Pipeline. We construct V-RAGBench through four stages: source video selection, event extraction and deduplication, candidate QA generation, and query filtering. Hour-scale egocentric videos are segmented into distinct event chunks, used to generate open-ended QA candidates, and then rigorously filtered for redundancy, answerability, shortcut bias, empirical answerability, and evidence uniqueness, yielding balanced set of uniquely grounded queries. Table 6: Dataset statistics detailing the distribution of 2,100 queries across categories, domains, and chunk durations, alongside 216 uncut source videos.

C.3 Definitions of the query categories The 2,100 queries are organized into three reasoning categories that target distinct capabilities of egocentric video understanding: Action Flow & Movement, which probes temporal and sequential reasoning; Object-Centric Visual Understanding, which focuses on fine-grained recognition of object attributes and states; and Interaction, which examines both physical manipulation and social engagement. Unlike Domestic, which is specific Ego4D activity domain, Daily refers to broader continuous life-logging episodes that may include household routines, desk work, leisure, transitions, and other ordinary activities across day. Videos of household or home-centered everyday activities, such as cooking, cleaning, organizing, eating, or moving around indoor living spaces. The key characteristic is routine domestic activity within home-like environment. Videos centered on hands-on physical manipulation, craftwork, repair, assembly, tool use, or laborintensive activities. The key characteristic is that the camera wearer is actively manipulating objects or materials. Videos involving interpersonal interaction, such as conversation, collaboration, instruction, assistance, or shared activity with other people. The key characteristic is that understanding the scene depends on social context, other peoples actions, or communicative behavior. Low-motion egocentric videos where the camera wearer remains largely fixed in place and attends to stable target over time, such as computer screen, television, desk, document, or workspace. The key characteristic is not the presence of screen itself, but the static first-person viewpoint and sustained attention to the same visual area. Videos captured in outdoor or large-scale physical environments, often involving walking, navigation, travel, sports, outdoor work, or other movement-heavy activities. The key characteristic is that the camera wearer interacts with changing external environment rather than fixed indoor workspace or household setting. sections.title: Description
sections.content: Questions requiring understanding of event sequences, movement, and scene transitions. These questions emphasizes temporal flow before, during, and after key actions. The expected answer must describe temporal or sequential fact. Questions that require recognizing specific details about objects: identity, visual attributes (color, shape, size), current state, or count. The expected answer must identify specific object detail. Questions about the camera wearers interaction with their environment, encompassing both the physical manipulation of objects (how things are held, used, or transferred) and social engagement (roles, relationships, and interpersonal behaviors). The expected answer must describe physical interaction with an object or social fact involving other people. C.4 Event selection and de-duplication details Event Chunk Construction Details. Section 4 describes the overall event extraction and deduplication procedure. Here we clarify two implementation consequences of this design. First, temporal segmentation and semantic deduplication play separate roles: segmentation determines valid timecontiguous event candidates, while deduplication only selects among those candidates and does not modify their temporal boundaries. Thus, each retained chunk remains an original contiguous video interval. Second, all event-level statistics are computed after deduplication. Since the retained chunks are selected as representatives of semantically similar segment groups, the final evidence pool is intentionally smaller and less redundant than the raw segmentation output. In particular, repeated daily routines are counted only through their selected representative chunks, which reduces the chance that generated query can be answered from multiple near-duplicate events in the same video. Because the representatives are not forced into fixed temporal length, the resulting chunks naturally span range of durations. We report the duration distribution of these deduplicated event chunks in Table 9 and Table 10. Table 9: Descriptive statistics of event chunk durations (in seconds) by dataset.

We therefore randomly sample 700 queries per category, yielding the final balanced set of 2,100 queries. Table 11: Filtering statistics for the combined dataset. sections.content: 67,370 52,783 28,331 19,751 11,041 5,907 14,587 (21.65%) 24,452 (36.30%) 8,580 (12.74%) 8,710 (12.93%) 5,134 (7.62%) 5,907 (8.77% kept) Semantic Similarity Filter. Event-level visual deduplication does not fully eliminate redundancy at the query level, since different retained events can still induce near-identical natural-language questions. We therefore apply this filter over query embeddings rather than event embeddings, removing candidates that are semantically too close to already retained queries. This reduces benchmark inflation from repeated phrasings of similar actions. Answerability Filter. For each candidate, we evaluate the paired (q, event clip) with GPT-5.2chat and ask whether the answer can be determined from the visual evidence. Candidates judged unanswerable from their source clip are discarded. Shortcut Bias Filter. Some generated queries can be answered from language priors or commonsense knowledge without inspecting the video. To identify such cases, we provide GPT-5.2-chat with the query alone, without any visual context, and compare its answer against the reference answer. If the blind answer matches the reference, the query is discarded as shortcut-biased. Table 12 shows examples of queries filtered as they exhibit shortcut bias. Empirical Answerability Filter. After the previous filters, we further check whether the query is practically answerable by strong VLLM using only the source evidence chunk. We run Gemini 2.5flash on the paired clip and evaluate the generated answer with the binary LLM-as-a-judge described in Appendix D.4. Queries that the model fails to answer correctly are removed, since they are unlikely to be reliably answerable in downstream evaluation. Evidence Uniqueness Filter. To test whether the annotated evidence is uniquely sufficient, we simulate retrieval over non-evidence clips from the same source video. Each video is split into 18 2-minute chunks, which are embedded with Qwen3-VL-8B. For every query, we retrieve the top-10 most similar chunks while explicitly excluding the chunk containing the true evidence. Gemini 2.5flash is then asked to answer using only this non-evidence retrieval set, and the output is judged with the same binary LLM-as-a-judge as in the empirical answerability filter. If the answer is still judged correct, the query is removed because its answer is recoverable from elsewhere in the video rather than uniquely grounded in the annotated evidence. Figure 3 presents the case study of queries whose evidence is not uniquely grounded in single evidence chunk. Figure 3: Examples of the queries not grounded with unique evidence chunk. Human Verification. To further validate the effectiveness of our answerability filters, we conduct human evaluation on the retained queries. Specifically, we recruit two graduate student annotators 19 Table 12: Examples of benchmark video queries with shortcut bias, which are answerable without access to the ground-truth source video. GPT-5.2-Chat resolved each query correctly from the question text alone, via common sense, contextual cues, or parametric knowledge. Q1: While am standing at the wooden island with the carton of milk and metal mixing bowls, what is the color of the liquid inside the clear plastic measuring cup? Blind prediction: White. GT: White. Why answerable: The question explicitly mentions carton of milk on the same surface as the measuring cup. Common sense strongly suggests the liquid being measured is the milk, making white inferable without watching the video. Q2: What visible item indicates the womans professional role while she sits and talks to me? Blind prediction: visible name badge or ID badge. GT: badge. Why answerable: The phrase visible item indicates professional role is near-defining description of name badge or ID badge. Parametric knowledge of workplace conventions makes this the default answer regardless of video content. Q3: Upon approaching the man sitting at the white table, how does the person acknowledge my presence? Blind prediction: He looks up and greets you (e.g., says hello or nods). GT: By greeting. Why answerable: Standard social conventions dictate that acknowledging an approaching person involves looking up and greeting them. The query frames the action as acknowledge, which already implies greeting-style response. and sample 577 QA pairs at random (corresponding to 95% confidence interval with margin of 4 over the full set of 2,100 queries). To ensure annotation quality, we only recruit annotators who have at least one first-author publication at top-tier AI conference and possess English proficiency equivalent to CEFR C2. Prior to annotation, each annotator received 30-minute instructional briefing on the task. Each annotator is then provided with the source video clip, the query, and the ground-truth answer, and is asked to judge whether the answer can be reasonably inferred from the video. We find that 96.9% of the sampled queries are judged as answerable by both annotators. The interannotator agreement (IAA) is 0.63 (Cohens Kappa [14]), indicating substantial agreement. These results further support that the retained queries are reliably answerable from their associated evidence.

sections.content: gemini-3-flash-preview QA generation in Section 4 gemini-2.5-flash Uniqueness, empirical answerability filtering in Section 4 gpt-5.2-chat-latest Answerability, Shortcut bias filter in Section 4 20 D.1 CARVE Implementations. Representation Construction. We split each video into non-overlapping 120-second segments and build four representations per segment, indexed identically across representations. The VF encodes 5 keyframes per segment; the VC encodes up to 64 frames per segment (maximum 1,843,200 pixels per frame, sampled at 1 fps). The TF view captions the 5 keyframes with Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct [5] up to 512 tokens; the TC view captions per-second frames at 1 fps. Retrieval and Generation. At inference, we retrieve the top-K=5 chunks per query, with reranking using the default Qwen3-VL-Reranker-2B [5]. The retrieved chunks are passed to Qwen3-VL-8BInstruct with maximum of 512 new tokens under its default decoding setup. Every generated answer is judged against the reference by binary judge (Qwen3.6-35B-A3B), using identical judge weights and prompt across our method and all baselines. D.2 Baseline Implementations System-level Baselines.VideoRAG-A [28] is retrieval method that encodes each video segment by linearly interpolating its textual and visual representations. We set α as 0.5 in this set up. GQR [59] refines the retrievers query embedding by gradient descent against mixture of the two retrievers score distributions, using KL minimization. We set two retrievers as text and video retrievers with learning rate 5e4. FreeRet [89] repurposes an MLLM (Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct [61] in our setup) as training-free two-stage retriever, extracting segment embeddings from the last self-attention sublayer and reranking the candidates via MCQ-based scoring. GME [85] is unified multimodal embedding model based on Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct [61]. We apply both methods directly to the raw video segments and query. Query-level Baselines.VideoRAG-B [42] prompts Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct [5] to decouple each query into three memory-specific sub-queries, retrieves from each memory, and merges the results before passing them to the generator. Following the original paper, we use one visual retriever and two text retrievers. RRF [50] is the RAG-Fusion variant of reciprocal rank fusion: the MLLM (Qwen3-VL8B-Instruct [5]) rewrites each query into two modality-targeted paraphrases one for text-clip, one for text-frames which drive two retrievers operating on the text-clip and text-frame embeddings, respectively. The resulting ranked lists are aggregated by the standard RRF formula with k=60. DAT [22] adaptively balances two retrievers per query using LLM. We set the papers dense and sparse setting with text clip and frames, α that interpolates the min-max-normalized similarities over the candidate union. UniversalRAG [76] routes each query to single optimal (m, g) configuration using trained classifier and applies the selected configuration uniformly to all retrieved chunks. We implement two variants-an LLM-based router (Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct fine-tuned with LoRA r=32, BCE multi-hot supervision) and lightweight encoder-based router (T5Gemma 2 270M [80] with linear classification head) and replace the papers sigmoid-thresholded decoding with argmax at inference for consistency with the top-K retrieval setting used by all other baselines. Router Implementations. To evaluate the benefits of chunk-level decisions, we compare CARVE against two types of query-level routers that select single configuration (m, g) per query. To train LLM-based Router, following the training recipe of UniversalRAG [76], we fine-tune Qwen3-VL-2BInstruct [5] as multi-label classifier over the four configurations. We apply LoRA (r = 32) and train for 5 epochs with 2e5 learning rate. Labels are derived from our train set, where configuration is labeled positive if it produces correct answer. At inference, we select the configuration with the highest predicted score (argmax) and apply it uniformly to all chunks for both retrieval and generation. To train encoder based router, we train an MLP-based classifier on top of frozen BGE-large-env1.5 [68] encoder. We use the frozen [CLS] representation as input to classification head consisting of three hidden blocks with size of 512. The model is optimized using binary cross-entropy with 1e3 learning rate. Similar to the LLM router, we use argmax at inference to determine single, query-uniform configuration. D.3 Evaluation Metrics We evaluate retrieval and generation separately. Let denote the set of evaluation queries. For each query Q, let Gq be the set of ground-truth evidence chunks annotated for q, and let 21 = [r1, r2, . . . , rK] be the ranked list of the top-K retrieved chunks. In our main experiments, we RK set = 5. Recall@K. Recall@K measures whether the ground-truth evidence appears in the top-K retrieved chunks. For query q, we define Recall@K(q) = RK Gq Gq . The dataset-level score is the average over all queries: Recall@K = 1 (cid:88) qQ Recall@K(q). The prompt used for judgment is shown in Figure 8. Human Verification. To assess the reliability of the LLM-as-a-judge, we conduct human validation of its outputs. We randomly sample 600 instances from the full set of 2,100 predictions (corresponding to 95% confidence interval with margin of error of 4). We apply the same annotator qualification criteria as in Section C.5, namely (i) graduate students in the field of AI, (ii) at least one first-author publication at top-tier AI conference, (iii) English proficiency equivalent to CEFR C2, and (iv) completion of 30-minute instructional briefing prior to annotation. Each annotator is then provided with the model-generated answer, the ground-truth answer, and the binary decision from the LLM-asa-judge indicating whether the prediction is correct, and is asked to independently assess whether the binary judges evaluation label should be considered correct. We find substantial agreement between human annotators and the LLM-as-a-judge, with Cohens Kappa score of 0.61. Further inspection shows that in 2.2% of the instances, both annotators agree that the LLM-as-a-judge made an incorrect decision. These results suggest that while assessing open-ended questions with binary LLM-as-a-judge may introduce minor noise, it can be considered generally reliable for large-scale evaluation.

sections.title: F Additional Analysis
sections.content: We conduct the additional analysis as follows: (i) comparison against reranked baselines to decouple the impact of chunk-level configuration selection, (ii) performance breakdown per source video to evaluate robustness across different video scales and (iii) an error analysis on the generation. F.1 Baseline with Reranking To verify that CARVEs gains are not solely attributable to the reranking stage, we apply all baselines with the same reranker used in our method. As illustrated in Table 15, while the addition of the reranker generally yields performance improvements, these gains remain marginal and, CARVE significantly outperforms even the reranked baseline. This result demonstrates that the advantage of CARVE does not merely stem from the reranking mechanism as standalone tool. Instead, it originates from the systems unique ability to select and interleave the most discriminative modality-granularity configurations at the chunk level. F.2 Performance Breakdown by Source Dataset To analyze the effectiveness of CARVE across different video scales,we specifically analyze its performance depending on the source dataset. Ego4D already presents challenging retrieval task with videos exceeding one hour, while EgoLife extends this challenge further with day-long recordings. sections.content: Retrieval Qwen3-VL-8B Qwen3-VL-32B Case 1 Case 2 Fail Hit 92 108 86 104 F.4 Effect of Top-K We also vary the final retrieval depth to test whether CARVE is sensitive to the number of chunks passed to the generator. This setting is important because increasing can improve recall by adding more candidate evidence, but it can also introduce distractor chunks that make generation harder. robust VideoRAG method should therefore maintain strong performance across different retrieval depths, rather than relying on single tuned value of k. Across the tested values of k, CARVE remains stable and consistently competitive, showing that its gains do not depend on particular retrieval budget. This indicates that chunk-wise parallel reranking improves the ordering of evidence itself, rather than merely benefiting from retrieving more chunks. In other words, CARVE is effective because it selects better-ranked evidence, not because it relies on larger context window. This experiment shows that chunk-adaptive reranking produces robust evidence ranking across retrieval depths, making CARVE less sensitive to the final top-k choice. F.5 Reranker Backbone Robustness Table 18 shows the retrieval performance per each representation configuration under different reranker backbone model (LamRA-Rank-7B.).
